channel.title: Responsible AI & Neurodivergence, Naba Rizvi
channel.description: Daily curated feed on responsible AI for neurodivergent people and serious mental illness.

item.title: AccessEval: Benchmarking Disability Bias in Large Language Models, EMNLP 2025
item.description: Oracle researchers test 21 open and closed models on paired neutral and disability-aware queries across six real-world domains and nine disability types. Responses to disability-aware prompts carry a more negative tone, more stereotyping, and higher factual error, with hearing, speech, and mobility disabilities hit hardest, reflecting persistent ableism embedded in model behavior.

item.title: Who's Asking? Investigating Bias Through the Lens of Disability-Framed Queries in LLMs, arXiv, 2025
item.description: The first systematic audit of disability-conditioned demographic bias across eight instruction-tuned models from 3B to 72B parameters. Pairing nine disability categories with six business domains, the study finds models infer gender, income, and education from phrasing alone, that disability cues measurably reshape those inferences, and that larger models keep answering demographic questions they should decline.

item.title: Who Gets Left Behind? Auditing Disability Inclusivity in Large Language Models, arXiv, 2025
item.description: A human-validated benchmark scores 17 models on the breadth, balance, and depth of their accessibility guidance across nine disability categories. Vision, hearing, and mobility are addressed well, while speech, genetic and developmental, sensory-cognitive, and mental health needs stay consistently underserved, revealing who current systems leave behind and pointing to taxonomy-aware training and evaluation.

item.title: The Impact of Disability Disclosure on Fairness and Bias in LLM-Driven Candidate Selection, FLAIRS 2025
item.description: Across five models and otherwise identical candidate profiles, LLMs consistently preferred applicants who disclosed no disability, and even penalized those who declined to answer relative to those who stated they had none. Model explanations assumed disabled candidates would need accommodations or lack confidence, showing how hiring stereotypes surface in automated selection.

item.title: From Verdicts to Maps: Reimagining AI Assessment for ADHD Through a Strength-Based Lens, AIED 2026
item.description: A design study warns of a precision paradox: high-confidence AI that quantifies ADHD deficits can harden pathological labeling and parental anxiety. Testing a strength-based assessment probe with parents and teachers, the authors show reframed visualizations shift assessment from anxiety-inducing verdicts toward collaborative understanding of neurodivergent strengths.
